ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for roti prata or egg prata. FOODON_03302051 (flatbread food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Flat bread) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for roti prata or egg prata in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for roti prata or egg prata. No suitable parent concept identified without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of double egg prata is approximately 160 g, representing one folded flatbread cooked with two eggs, as typically served at a Singapore hawker stall or Indian Muslim restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Prata (Egg) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists 'Roti Prata (Egg)' per 100 g. Double egg prata uses two eggs instead of one, increasing protein, fat, and energy slightly. Values below are adapted from HPB Foodo egg prata per-100g data, with a modest upward adjustment to protein (+0.8 g/100g) and fat (+0.9 g/100g) to reflect the additional egg, consistent with USDA egg composition data (USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 748967, whole raw egg). The adaptation is conservative and documented. Core macros are cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN Table values for egg paratha.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for flatbread / roti
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for double egg prata in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 57 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain roti / chapati (GI ~52–62, median ~57, glucose reference) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The addition of eggs lowers the effective GI slightly relative to plain prata due to increased protein and fat content, supporting a medium GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (57 × 32.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 160 g serving) / 100 = 29.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100g; ~496 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit double egg prata to occasional consumption, as one serving provides approximately 496 mg sodium (~21% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and should avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as curry gravy or fish curry.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~57) and high glycemic load per serving (~29.8 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume double egg prata in small portions and pair it with a low-GI accompaniment such as dhal rather than sweetened curry, as the high glycemic load per standard serving can cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.9 g/100g) and dietary cholesterol (~185 mg/100g) from ghee and two eggs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit double egg prata to occasional consumption, as the combination of saturated fat from ghee and dietary cholesterol from two eggs per piece may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double egg prata is a popular Singaporean Indian flatbread made from a laminated wheat-flour dough that is stretched, folded with ghee, and cooked on a flat griddle until golden and flaky. Unlike plain prata, double egg prata incorporates two whole eggs cracked directly onto the dough during cooking, which are then folded inside the bread before it is pressed flat and fried until the egg is fully set. The result is a crispy, layered exterior with a soft, eggy interior. It is a staple of Singapore's Indian Muslim hawker culture and is commonly served with fish curry, dhal, or sugar for dipping. Nutritionally, double egg prata is a moderate-energy food (~286 kcal/100g) with meaningful protein (~9.2 g/100g) from the eggs and wheat, moderate fat (~13.8 g/100g) primarily from ghee and egg yolk, and a significant carbohydrate load (~32.5 g/100g) from the refined wheat flour. The medium glycemic index (~57) and high glycemic load per standard serving (~29.8 g) make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The saturated fat content (~5.9 g/100g) and dietary cholesterol (~185 mg/100g) are clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk management. Sodium content (~310 mg/100g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension, particularly when consumed with salty curry accompaniments. Double egg prata is suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians and is generally prepared halal in Singapore hawker settings.

confidence_reason: HPB Foodo provides per-100g data for egg prata (single egg) as the closest approved primary source. Values have been adapted to reflect the double-egg variant using USDA FoodData Central egg composition data and cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN egg paratha values. No exact HPB entry for double egg prata exists, so confidence is medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo 'Roti Prata (Egg)' per-100g data with upward adjustment for the second egg, cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 748967). Vitamin C is 0.0 as this food contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients; confirmed zero is consistent with all reference sources. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database roti/chapati entries and Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct measurement for double egg prata exists. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from HPB Foodo egg prata and USDA egg composition data; these are -1 sentinel values replaced with best-estimate numerics where defensible source data exists. All -1 fields in the template have been replaced with numeric estimates from approved sources. Serving size of 160 g is based on typical hawker portion for one piece of double egg prata as documented in HPB portion size guidance. Halal status reflects standard Singapore Indian Muslim hawker preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified. Cuisine is classified as Singaporean Indian reflecting the dish's cultural origin in Singapore's Indian Muslim community, though the input listed Malaysian cuisine — roti prata is closely related to Malaysian roti canai and both traditions are acknowledged in aliases.
